raw_case:
Corpus callosal agenesis with gray matter heterotopia and bilateral eye coloboma in an infant: A case report
Corpus callosal agenesis (CCA) is a rare congenital disorder characterized by the partial or complete absence of the corpus callosum, a structure crucial for interhemispheric communication. CCA can occur in isolation or be associated with other anomalies such as heterotopia, holoprosencephaly, cerebellar hypoplasia, coloboma, and hydrocephalus. The prevalence of CCA ranges from 0.020% to 0.025%, though some reports suggest higher rates. This case report describes a 1-year-old male with developmental delays and no significant antenatal or family history. MRI revealed a complete absence of the corpus callosum, asymmetrically dilated lateral ventricles, subependymal gray matter nodules suggestive of gray matter heterotopia, and bilateral posterior globe defects with vitreous herniation, indicating severe ocular anomalies. The child received supportive care including physical therapy and special education services, with regular follow-ups for developmental and ophthalmologic evaluation. This case report details the rare occurrence of CCA, accompanied by gray matter heterotopia and bilateral posterior eye coloboma in a pediatric patient. The combination of these congenital anomalies presents unique diagnostic and management challenges requiring multidisciplinary care. We discuss the clinical presentation, radiological findings, and implications for supportive care and improving the prognosis.

Introduction

Corpus callosal agenesis (CCA) is a rare congenital disorder characterized by the partial or complete absence of the corpus callosum, the structure that connects the 2 cerebral hemispheres. It can occur in isolation or in association with other anomalies, including heterotopia, holoprosencephaly, cerebellar hypoplasia, coloboma, abnormal cerebral fissures, porencephalic cysts, and hydrocephalus [1]. Heterotopia refers to an abnormal collection of neurons in an anomalous location other than the cortical gray matter. It is believed to be secondary to an arrested migration of neurons from the germinal matrix toward the cortical ribbon along the radial glial path [2]. Coloboma is a developmental anomaly that results from incomplete closure of the embryonic choroidal fissure, resulting in ectasia and herniation of vitreous into the retro-ocular space [3]. This defect in ocular structure may coexist with CCA but is rarely reported together. The prevalence of congenital absence of corpus callosum ranges from 1 in 5000 to 1 in 4000 (0.020%-0.025%), with some reports indicating a higher prevalence of 0.2%-0.7% [4,5].This case report aims to contribute to the limited literature on this combination of anomalies, emphasizing the importance of thorough radiological and clinical evaluation in the diagnosis and management of similarly affected patients.

Case presentation

A 1-year-old male presented to the pediatric neurology clinic with a history of developmental delay. The patient's parents reported that the child was not developing as per his peers. The child's antenatal, natal, and postnatal history were uneventful. His mother did not consume alcohol or other illicit drugs during pregnancy. Furthermore, there was no family history of developmental delays. On examination, the patient exhibited normocephaly, normal tone in extremities, and without dysmorphic facial features. Vital signs were within normal limits. On assessment, the patient displayed global developmental delays, i.e. not being able to sit without support and not being able to speak even a single word, apart from coos and babbles.His biochemical and hematologic investigations were within normal limits. Therefore, imaging with MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) brain was sought to look for any structural defects. A T1-weighted sagittal MRI image showed a complete absence of the corpus callosum (Fig. 1). This finding was further corroborated by a T2-weighted coronal image, which confirmed the absence of the corpus callosum, but distinct Probst bundles, indicative of re-routed white matter tracts, and also revealed asymmetrically dilated lateral ventricles (Fig. 2). Additionally, T2-weighted axial images displayed subependymal gray matter nodules along the right lateral ventricular wall, indicative of gray matter heterotopia (Fig. 3). Furthermore, axial T1-weighted images identified bilateral defects in the posterior globe, with herniation of the vitreous humor through these defects, suggesting severe ocular anomalies (Figs. 4A and B).The patient's parents were counseled about the findings of imaging. The patient was managed with conservative and supportive measures including physical therapy, and special education services. Regular follow-ups were scheduled to monitor developmental progress. Ophthalmologic follow-ups were also arranged to look for visual impairment and potential complications related to coloboma.

Discussion

CCA refers to the partial or complete absence of the corpus callosum, the major commissural structure connecting the 2 cerebral hemispheres. This condition may be congenital or acquired and can manifest in isolation or alongside other neurological anomalies, including heterotopia, microgyria, and hydrocephalus [1,6]. Embryologically, the corpus callosum forms between the eighth and 20th weeks of gestation, and its agenesis is often linked to complex telencephalic or posterior fossa malformations [7]. During this time the corpus callosum forms through a sequence of developmental steps, including midline patterning and axon guidance across the hemispheres [8].Structural brain abnormalities, including CCA, occur in approximately 1 to 2 per 1000 births, with genetic disorders, being key contributors [9]. Estimates suggest it occurs in approximately 1.8 per 10,000 in the general population, with higher rates of 230-600 per 10,000 in children with neurodevelopmental disabilities [10]. CCA is notably more prevalent in males, and its detection often occurs within the first year of life, especially when associated with other neurological or cognitive deficits [11]. Our case was also male in the first year of life when the CCA got detected.The etiology of CCA is complex and multifactorial. It reflects a mix of genetic and environmental factors, with up to 70% of cases having an unclear cause even after comprehensive clinical evaluation [12,13]. While genetic factors account for 30–45% of CCA cases, with 10% linked to genetic mutations and chromosomal abnormalities such as trisomies 18, 13, and 8 [1,14], environmental factors also play a significant role [15]. Genetic defects, such as haploinsufficiency of the AKT3 gene, have also been identified as contributing factors, particularly in cases associated with microcephaly [16]. Fetal alcohol syndrome, advanced maternal age, premature birth, maternal diabetes, and infections like rubella have all been implicated in CCA development [17,18]. However, our patient did not have in-utero exposure to any identifiable insults. His mother was nondiabetic and did not have infections. Chromosomal microarray analysis (CMA) and exome sequencing can help identify the genetic basis of CCA in some cases [14] though it was not pursued in our case due to unavailability.Clinical manifestations of CCA vary widely, ranging from severe developmental delays to being asymptomatic, depending on the presence of other abnormalities and the extent of the agenesis [15]. Our patient had associated heterotopia and presented with global developmental delay, which is in line with the established literature. The absence of the corpus callosum disrupts interhemispheric communication, which is crucial for sensory, motor, and cognitive integration [19]. Agenesis can lead to developmental delays and seizure disorders, particularly in cases associated with heterotopia [6]. However, no seizures were presented in our case.CCA is associated with over 100 congenital syndromes [19]. It is often accompanied by other central nervous system (CNS) abnormalities, including Dandy-Walker malformation, holoprosencephaly, and cerebellar hypoplasia. Our case has heterotopia and bilateral coloboma in eye. Bilateral involvement often indicates a more widespread developmental issue, which may be associated with syndromes such as CHARGE syndrome. These associations highlight the disrupted early cerebral development processes leading to CCA. Structural anomalies such as interhemispheric cysts, hydrocephalus, and cortical malformations are common in CCA, reflecting the malformation's role as part of broader syndromic conditions like Aicardi syndrome and Andermann syndrome [14,20]. Additionally, extracerebral abnormalities may affect the eyes, heart, and kidneys, indicating a systemic developmental issue [19,21]. However, our patient did not have any facial or other characteristics to suggest syndromic occurrences.Diagnosis of CCA is primarily established through neuroimaging, often prompted by evaluations for developmental delay or epilepsy [7]. Ultrasound is useful for early detection, but its ability to fully assess CCA is limited compared to MRI, and it is best used as a preliminary screening tool [19]. Ultrasound findings associated with CCA include absence of the CSP(Cavum Septi Pellucidi), teardrop-shaped lateral ventricles with enlarged occipital horns and reduced frontal horn size, Increased separation of the lateral ventricles and upward displacement of the third ventricle [14,22]. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), particularly in the sagittal plane, is the preferred method for diagnosing CCA, as in our case, due to its ability to visualize the brain in multiple planes, assessing associated anomalies, and a low false positive rate compared to USG [23]. MRI findings for CCA include the absence of the corpus callosum, an elevated third ventricle, and the presence of Probst's bundles [6]. The presence of Probst bundles, which are longitudinally oriented neuronal fibers, is a hallmark of CCA, indicating abnormal commissure formation [7,15,24]. These typical findings are present in the MRI imaging of our case, except for elevated third ventricles. CT is less favored than MRI due to lower resolution and limitations in detecting partial agenesis, but it is an alternative when MRI is unavailable [1].Treatment and prognosis for CCA vary widely, particularly because CCA often occurs with other brain anomalies. There is no universal treatment for all cases; instead, interventions are tailored to individual needs. Effective management of CCA involves a multidisciplinary approach, including genetic counseling, early intervention programs, and ongoing neurodevelopmental assessments to support optimal outcomes [25]. Common therapies include physical therapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy, with early intervention crucial for addressing developmental delays [18]. Regular monitoring and support are recommended to address developmental and educational needs as they arise [18,21]. Genetic counseling and prenatal imaging are essential in assessing recurrence risks and planning for future pregnancies [25].Children with CCA present with a broad spectrum of neurodevelopmental outcomes. Approximately 76.04% of children prenatally diagnosed with isolated complete CCA exhibit normal neurodevelopment, though developmental delays and other issues are frequently reported by parents [15]. However, isolated CCA, particularly when identified incidentally, often carries a better prognosis, with many individuals showing no significant neurological symptoms [6,10].

Conclusion

Agenesis of the corpus callosum may present with diverse clinical manifestations influenced by multiple factors. The patient's presentation of CAA with heterotopia and coloboma on MRI underscores the rarity and complexity of such cases, adding to the understanding of clinicians about active evaluation of associated anomalies in the case of corpus callosum agenesis. The condition's clinical presentation, genetic diversity, and association with other brain anomalies underscore the need for comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and management tailored to each case.

Provenance And peer review

Not commissioned, externally peer-reviewed.

Authors contributions

Saubhagya Dhakal, Saroj Kumar Jha, Alisha Adhikari: Led data collection, and contributed to writing the case information. Saubhagya Dhakal, Saroj Kumar Jha, Alisha Adhikari: Contributed to the process of original draft preparation and introduction and discussion. Saroj Kumar Jha, Alisha Adhikari, Pinky Jha, Srijana Katwal: Contributed to conceptualization and discussion. Saubhagya Dhakal, Saroj Kumar Jha, Alisha Adhikari, Pinky Jha, Srijana Katwal: Revised it critically for important intellectual content, and contributed in review and editing. Saroj Kumar Jha, Alisha Adhikari: Edited the rough draft into the final manuscript.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was taken from the patient for the publication of the case report and the images. A copy of it is available for review by the editor-in-chief of this journal on request.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 1-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Developmental delay
- **History of Present Illness:** The patient was not meeting developmental milestones for his age, including not sitting without support or speaking apart from coos and babbles.
- **Past Medical History:** None; antenatal, natal, and postnatal history were uneventful.
- **Personal History:** No maternal exposure to alcohol, illicit drugs, diabetes, or infections during pregnancy.
- **Family History:** No history of developmental delays.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Normocephaly
  - Normal tone in extremities
  - No dysmorphic facial features
  - Global developmental delay
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Biochemical and hematologic results: Within normal limits.
  - MRI Findings:
    - Complete absence of the corpus callosum.
    - Asymmetrically dilated lateral ventricles.
    - Subependymal gray matter nodules indicating gray matter heterotopia.
    - Bilateral posterior globe defects with herniation of the vitreous humor, confirming coloboma.
- **Management:** Supportive care including physical therapy and special education services. Regular developmental and ophthalmological follow-ups.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Corpus Callosal Agenesis (CCA):**  
   - **Rationale:** The MRI findings of the complete absence of the corpus callosum, along with Probst bundles and abnormal lateral ventricles, are diagnostic.  
   - **Why Included:** The absence of the corpus callosum on imaging is definitive, supported by the clinical presentation of global developmental delay.  

2. **Associated Anomalies – Heterotopia and Coloboma:**  
   - **Rationale:** Subependymal gray matter nodules were detected on MRI, confirming gray matter heterotopia. Bilateral posterior globe defects and vitreous herniation indicate coloboma, commonly linked to developmental anomalies like CCA.  
   - **Why Included:** These anomalies were identified on imaging and are known associations with CCA.  

3. **Other Syndromic Associations (e.g., CHARGE syndrome):**  
   - **Rationale:** CCA is often linked with broader syndromic conditions like CHARGE or Dandy-Walker malformation.  
   - **Why Excluded:** The patient lacked physical or clinical features (e.g., craniofacial or systemic abnormalities) to support a specific syndromic diagnosis. No genetic or chromosomal analysis was performed to investigate further, but no evident syndromic markers were noted.  

4. **Seizure Disorders:**  
   - **Rationale:** CCA with gray matter heterotopia often leads to seizures due to disrupted brain connectivity.  
   - **Why Excluded:** The patient had no history of seizures up to the age of 1 year.

The final diagnosis focused on the structural abnormalities (CCA, heterotopia, coloboma) identified on imaging, as the clinical presentation and developmental delays correlated with these findings while excluding syndromic and other neurological conditions due to the absence of supportive evidence.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with **Corpus Callosal Agenesis**, associated with **subependymal gray matter heterotopia** and **bilateral eye coloboma**. These findings explain his global developmental delay and were confirmed by MRI imaging. Supportive care and regular follow-up were implemented based on his needs.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Corpus callosal agenesis with subependymal gray matter heterotopia and bilateral eye coloboma

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's information. He's a 1-year-old male with developmental delay as the chief complaint. The history says he's not sitting without support and not speaking beyond coos and babbles. So, developmental milestones delayed in motor and language areas. 

Now, his past medical history is unremarkable, no complications during pregnancy or birth. The mother didn't have any infections, diabetes, or substance use. Family history also doesn't mention any developmental issues. So probably not an inherited condition from the family, but maybe a de novo genetic mutation?

Physical exam shows normocephaly, normal tone, no dysmorphic features. But global developmental delay. So the delay is across multiple domains. The MRI findings are key here. The corpus callosum is completely absent—that's agenesis of the corpus callosum. Then there's asymmetrical dilated lateral ventricles, which could be due to the missing corpus callosum or other structural issues.

Subependymal gray matter nodules suggest gray matter heterotopia. That's a neuronal migration disorder, where neurons didn't move to the right place during brain development. Then there's bilateral posterior globe defects with herniation of the vitreous, which is coloboma. Coloboma is an eye defect that can be part of certain syndromes.

Putting this together: agenesis of the corpus callosum, gray matter heterotopia, coloboma. These three features are classic for a specific genetic syndrome. The triad makes me think of Aicardi syndrome. Wait, but Aicardi is typically seen in females, since it's X-linked dominant and usually lethal in males. However, there might be rare cases in males with Klinefelter (XXY) or somatic mosaicism. Alternatively, could it be another condition like CHARGE syndrome? But CHARGE includes coloboma, heart defects, atresia choanae, growth retardation, etc. Not sure if that fits here.

Wait, the patient's MRI shows gray matter heterotopia, which is a neuronal migration issue. Aicardi syndrome is also associated with chorioretinal lacunae and infantile spasms. But the eye finding here is coloboma. Maybe another syndrome? Let me think. Another possibility is septo-optic dysplasia, but that typically involves optic nerve hypoplasia, pituitary issues, and midline brain defects. The presence of heterotopia and coloboma might point elsewhere.

Wait, the combination of agenesis of the corpus callosum, heterotopia, and coloboma—maybe it's a tubulinopathy? Some tubulin gene mutations can cause brain malformations. But the eye coloboma is a key feature here. Alternatively, could it be a ciliopathy? Some ciliopathies have coloboma and brain abnormalities. 

Wait, another syndrome: Aicardi-Goutières syndrome? No, that's more about leukoencephalopathy and interferon signaling. Maybe not. Let's go back. Agenesis of the corpus callosum with interhemispheric cysts and coloboma—could that be another syndrome like Acrocallosal syndrome? But that includes polydactyly and other features not mentioned here.

Wait, another approach: the triad of agenesis of corpus callosum, coloboma, and heterotopia. Aicardi syndrome classically includes corpus callosum agenesis, chorioretinal lacunae (not coloboma), and infantile spasms. But in this case, the eye finding is coloboma. Hmm. Maybe a different genetic condition. For example, mutations in the ARX gene can cause brain malformations and other issues, but not sure about coloboma. 

Wait, the presence of gray matter heterotopia suggests a neuronal migration disorder. Coloboma is a structural eye defect. Agenesis of corpus callosum is a midline defect. Putting these together, could it be a PAX6 mutation? PAX6 is involved in eye development. But PAX6 mutations usually cause aniridia, not coloboma. 

Alternatively, the combination of ACC, coloboma, and heterotopia might point to a syndrome like Mowat-Wilson syndrome, which includes Hirschsprung disease and distinct facial features, which the patient doesn't have. 

Wait, the key here is the combination. Let me check if ACC, coloboma, and heterotopia are part of a known syndrome. Maybe a rare genetic disorder like microphthalmia with linear skin defects (MLS) syndrome, but that's more common in females and includes skin abnormalities. 

Alternatively, could this be a case of Aicardi syndrome in a male? Though rare, there have been a few reported cases of males with Aicardi-like features due to mosaicism or other genetic mechanisms. The presence of coloboma instead of chorioretinal lacunae might complicate that. 

Alternatively, the combination of ACC and coloboma is part of CHARGE syndrome. Let me recall CHARGE criteria: Coloboma, Heart defects, Atresia choanae, Retarded growth, Genital and Ear anomalies. The patient here has coloboma and developmental delay. But other features like heart defects or choanal atresia aren't mentioned. So maybe not enough for CHARGE. 

Another possibility is a tubulinopathy. Mutations in tubulin genes (e.g., TUBA1A) can cause brain malformations including ACC, heterotopia, and sometimes eye anomalies. Coloboma could be part of that spectrum. So tubulinopathy could be a possibility. 

Alternatively, could it be a form of lissencephaly? No, heterotopia is different from lissencephaly. 

Wait, the patient's MRI shows subependymal gray matter nodules (heterotopia) which are periventricular. That's a type of nodular heterotopia. The combination of heterotopia, ACC, and coloboma. 

Looking up possible syndromes, I recall that ACC with coloboma and heterotopia can be seen in certain genetic disorders. For example, mutations in the DCC gene are associated with ACC, but usually not with coloboma. 

Wait, another angle: the combination of ACC, coloboma, and heterotopia. The coloboma is posterior, so maybe part of a midline defect. 

Another possible condition is Joubert syndrome, which involves cerebellar abnormalities (molar tooth sign), but the MRI here doesn't mention that. 

Alternatively, could it be a ciliopathy like Bardet-Biedl syndrome? But that includes polydactyly, obesity, etc. Not mentioned here. 

Wait, another thought: the triad of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma. Could this be a case of a COL4A1 mutation? These can cause brain abnormalities, eye defects, and other issues. But the typical findings are porencephaly or hemorrhages. Not sure. 

Alternatively, the combination could be part of a syndrome caused by a mutation in the ARFGEF2 gene, which is associated with periventricular heterotopia, microcephaly, but not sure about coloboma. 

Hmm. Maybe it's time to look for a syndrome that combines ACC, coloboma, and heterotopia. Maybe Aicardi syndrome, despite being more common in females. Let's review the diagnostic criteria for Aicardi syndrome. The classic triad is agenesis of the corpus callosum, chorioretinal lacunae, and infantile spasms. However, some variants may have other eye anomalies. If the coloboma in this case is part of the spectrum, and considering the heterotopia (which can be part of Aicardi), perhaps this is a case of Aicardi syndrome. But the sex is male. Since Aicardi is X-linked dominant, males are rarely affected unless they have XXY or somatic mosaicism. However, this patient's gender is male, so if this is Aicardi, it would be very rare. Alternatively, perhaps another diagnosis. 

Another possibility is that this is a case of ACC with coloboma and heterotopia without a known syndrome. However, given the combination of findings, it's more likely a genetic syndrome. 

Wait, let's think again: the MRI shows ACC, gray matter heterotopia, and coloboma. The combination of ACC and coloboma can be part of a number of conditions. If we add heterotopia, that's more specific. 

Another syndrome: Microphthalmia, syndromic 12 (MCOPS12), which is caused by mutations in the RARB gene. It includes microphthalmia, coloboma, brain malformations. But not sure about heterotopia. 

Alternatively, the combination could be part of a tubulinopathy. Tubulinopathies can present with a range of brain malformations, including ACC, cortical malformations like heterotopia, and ocular colobomas. For example, TUBA1A mutations are associated with ACC, lissencephaly, and sometimes eye abnormalities. However, this patient has heterotopia instead of lissencephaly. 

Wait, another gene: TUBB3. Mutations here can cause congenital fibrosis of the extraocular muscles with brain malformations, but I'm not sure about coloboma. 

Alternatively, the combination of findings could be part of a ciliopathy. For example, OFD1 (oral-facial-digital syndrome type 1) can have ACC and polycystic kidneys, but coloboma isn't typical. 

Alternatively, Matthew-Wood syndrome is characterized by microphthalmia and agenesis of the corpus callosum, but again, not sure about heterotopia. 

Alternatively, perhaps a chromosomal microdeletion or duplication. For example, 1q43-q44 deletion includes ACC, but other features may vary. 

Alternatively, the patient's findings could be due to a de novo mutation in a gene associated with these features. For example, the DCC gene, which is involved in corpus callosum development. But I don't recall DCC mutations causing coloboma. 

Wait, I think I need to look up which genetic conditions present with agenesis of corpus callosum, coloboma, and gray matter heterotopia. Let me think through possible differentials again. 

Aicardi syndrome: ACC, chorioretinal lacunae (which are different from coloboma), infantile spasms. In this case, the eye finding is coloboma. So maybe not. 

CHARGE syndrome: Coloboma is a major criterion. Other major criteria include choanal atresia, cranial nerve abnormalities, ear anomalies. The patient here doesn't have those. So maybe not CHARGE. 

Tubulinopathy: Some tubulinopathies can present with ACC, cortical malformations (like heterotopia), and ocular colobomas. For example, TUBA1A mutations have been associated with ACC and ocular colobomas in some cases. If this is the case, then the diagnosis would be a tubulin-related disorder. 

Another possibility is a mutation in the COL4A1 or COL4A2 genes, which can cause brain abnormalities and eye defects. For example, COL4A1 mutations can lead to porencephaly, infantile hemiparesis, and ocular anomalies like coloboma. However, the MRI here shows heterotopia, not porencephaly. 

Wait, the presence of heterotopia (a neuronal migration defect) along with ACC and coloboma might point towards a defect in neuronal migration and midline development. Genes involved in both processes could be implicated. For example, the ARX gene is involved in neuronal migration and corpus callosum development. Mutations in ARX can cause X-linked lissencephaly with ambiguous genitalia (XLAG), but coloboma isn't typically part of that. 

Alternatively, the SOX2 gene is associated with anophthalmia/microphthalmia and brain abnormalities, but again, the eye finding here is coloboma. 

Wait, another syndrome: MIDAS (Microphthalmia, Dermal Aplasia, and Sclerocornea) syndrome, which is X-linked, but again, the features don't align. 

Alternatively, could this be a case of a PTEN mutation? PTEN mutations are associated with macrocephaly and other issues, but not these findings. 

Hmm. This is tricky. Let's recap the key findings: 

- Agenesis of corpus callosum
- Gray matter heterotopia (subependymal nodules)
- Bilateral coloboma
- Global developmental delay

Given these, the combination is suggestive of a genetic syndrome affecting midline brain development, neuronal migration, and eye development. 

Looking up "agenesis corpus callosum coloboma heterotopia" in my mind, I recall that some cases of ACC with coloboma and heterotopia are associated with mutations in the KIF7 gene, which is involved in ciliopathies. Joubert syndrome and related disorders (JSRD) can have coloboma, but typically with the molar tooth sign on MRI, which isn't mentioned here. 

Alternatively, the combination could be a variant of a tubulinopathy. For example, TUBA1A mutations can lead to a spectrum of brain malformations, including ACC and cortical malformations (like heterotopia or lissencephaly), and some reports mention eye abnormalities such as microphthalmia or coloboma. 

If that's the case, then the diagnosis would be a tubulinopathy due to a TUBA1A mutation or similar. 

Alternatively, another possibility is a mutation in the DCC gene, which is crucial for corpus callosum development. However, DCC mutations usually don't involve heterotopia or coloboma. 

Another thought: the presence of heterotopia and coloboma could suggest a defect in the same pathway that affects both neuronal migration and eye development. For example, the PAX genes are involved in both eye development and brain development. PAX6 mutations cause aniridia and brain abnormalities, but not typically coloboma or heterotopia. 

Alternatively, the CHD7 gene (associated with CHARGE syndrome) – but the patient doesn't meet enough criteria for CHARGE. 

Wait, given that the patient's MRI shows coloboma and ACC, along with heterotopia, and considering that tubulinopathies can present with these features, the most plausible diagnosis might be a tubulinopathy, such as a TUBA1A mutation. 

Alternatively, if the coloboma is a key feature, and given the other findings, perhaps a diagnosis of Aicardi syndrome, even though it's rare in males. However, the eye findings in Aicardi are typically chorioretinal lacunae, not coloboma. 

Alternatively, maybe this is a new or rare genetic syndrome not yet well characterized. But given the options, the most likely answer here is Aicardi syndrome, even though the patient is male. But given the absence of infantile spasms and the presence of coloboma instead of chorioretinal lacunae, that seems less likely. 

Wait, another approach. Agenesis of corpus callosum can occur in isolation or as part of a syndrome. The presence of additional brain abnormalities (heterotopia) and eye defects (coloboma) strongly suggests a syndromic cause. 

The combination of ACC and coloboma is seen in several syndromes, including CHARGE, but as mentioned earlier, the patient doesn't meet enough criteria. 

Another syndrome to consider is acrocallosal syndrome (ACLS), which features ACC, polydactyly, and developmental delay. But there's no mention of polydactyly here. 

Wait, the presence of gray matter heterotopia is a key clue. Heterotopia is a neuronal migration disorder. So conditions that affect neuronal migration and also cause ACC and coloboma. 

Tubulinopathies are a group of disorders caused by mutations in tubulin genes, leading to abnormal brain development. TUBA1A mutations, for example, are associated with a range of brain malformations, including ACC, lissencephaly, pachygyria, and less commonly, heterotopia. Ocular colobomas have been reported in some cases. 

Another gene, TUBB2B, is associated with asymmetric ventriculomegaly, heterotopia, and polymicrogyria. Some cases also have ACC and eye abnormalities. 

Given the MRI findings here—ACC, asymmetrically dilated lateral ventricles, heterotopia, and coloboma—a tubulinopathy seems plausible. 

Therefore, the final diagnosis here is likely a tubulinopathy, such as a TUBA1A or TUBB2B mutation. However, the prompt asks for a specific syndrome or diagnosis. If the options are limited to known syndromes, but the combination here doesn't fit a classic syndrome, then perhaps the best answer is ACC with coloboma and heterotopia secondary to a tubulinopathy. 

Alternatively, given the presence of coloboma and ACC, another possibility is a septo-optic dysplasia (SOD) variant, but SOD usually involves optic nerve hypoplasia, which isn't mentioned here. 

Wait, the coloboma is posterior globe with vitreous herniation. Colobomas can occur in various parts of the eye. In the context of ACC and heterotopia, the combination might be specific enough. 

After considering all possibilities, the most fitting diagnosis, considering the triad of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma, along with the other findings, is Aicardi Syndrome. Although typically female, there are rare male cases. However, the eye findings here are coloboma rather than chorioretinal lacunae. Alternatively, the presence of coloboma might indicate a different syndrome. 

Another possibility is that this is a case of a ciliopathy. For example, Joubert syndrome can have coloboma, but the MRI doesn't show the molar tooth sign. 

Alternatively, it's possible that this combination is indicative of a new genetic mutation not yet well-described. However, given the options available in the clinical setting and the need to choose the most likely diagnosis, the presence of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma in a male infant with developmental delay points towards a genetic disorder affecting neuronal migration and midline development. 

After reviewing all possibilities, the most probable diagnosis here is Aicardi Syndrome, considering the corpus callosum agenesis and heterotopia, despite the male gender and coloboma instead of chorioretinal lacunae. However, this might be a stretch. 

Alternatively, if not Aicardi, then a tubulinopathy. Since the answer requires a specific diagnosis, I think the key findings are the triad of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma. Upon checking, some sources mention that Aicardi syndrome can present with coloboma in rare cases, especially in males. Alternatively, another possibility is a tubulinopathy. 

Given the presence of gray matter heterotopia (a neuronal migration defect) and ACC, along with coloboma, the most accurate diagnosis is likely a tubulinopathy. However, if the answer is expecting a named syndrome, then perhaps the answer is Aicardi Syndrome, but I'm not entirely sure. 

Wait, another angle: Aicardi Syndrome is characterized by the triad of ACC, chorioretinal lacunae, and infantile spasms. This patient has coloboma, not lacunae, and no mention of seizures. So maybe Aicardi is less likely. 

In that case, considering the heterotopia and coloboma, the answer might be "X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia" but that's not fitting. 

Alternatively, the combination of ACC, coloboma, and heterotopia is part of the "ACCEPTS" syndrome (Agenesis of Corpus Callosum, Coloboma, Epilepsy, and other features), but that's not a recognized syndrome. 

Alternatively, the patient has ACC with coloboma and heterotopia, which could be due to a mutation in a gene like KIF7 or other ciliopathy genes.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient exhibits global developmental delay, with key structural abnormalities on MRI: complete agenesis of the corpus callosum (ACC), gray matter heterotopia, and bilateral coloboma. These findings suggest a congenital syndrome affecting midline brain development, neuronal migration, and ocular structures.  
<step 2> Aicardi syndrome, classically characterized by ACC, chorioretinal lacunae, and infantile spasms, is considered. However, the patient’s coloboma (instead of lacunae) and lack of seizures reduce its likelihood. Additionally, Aicardi syndrome is X-linked dominant and rare in males.  
<step 3> CHARGE syndrome is evaluated but dismissed due to the absence of choanal atresia, cranial nerve anomalies, or ear defects.  
<step 4> Tubulinopathies (e.g., TUBA1A/TUBB2B mutations) are strongly considered, as they are associated with ACC, heterotopia, and ocular colobomas. These disorders disrupt neuronal migration and microtubule function, aligning with the patient’s structural brain and eye abnormalities.  
<step 5> Other syndromes (e.g., septo-optic dysplasia, ciliopathies) are ruled out due to insufficient clinical overlap. The triad of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma most strongly supports a tubulinopathy.  

### Answer:
Tubulinopathy (e.g., TUBA1A-related disorder)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient exhibits global developmental delay, with key structural abnormalities on MRI: complete agenesis of the corpus callosum (ACC), gray matter heterotopia, and bilateral coloboma. These findings suggest a congenital syndrome affecting midline brain development, neuronal migration, and ocular structures.

Aicardi syndrome, classically characterized by ACC, chorioretinal lacunae, and infantile spasms, is considered. However, the patient’s coloboma (instead of lacunae) and lack of seizures reduce its likelihood. Additionally, Aicardi syndrome is X-linked dominant and rare in males.
CHARGE syndrome is evaluated but dismissed due to the absence of choanal atresia, cranial nerve anomalies, or ear defects.

Tubulinopathies (e.g., TUBA1A/TUBB2B mutations) are strongly considered, as they are associated with ACC, heterotopia, and ocular colobomas. These disorders disrupt neuronal migration and microtubule function, aligning with the patient’s structural brain and eye abnormalities.
Other syndromes (e.g., septo-optic dysplasia, ciliopathies) are ruled out due to insufficient clinical overlap. The triad of ACC, heterotopia, and coloboma most strongly supports a tubulinopathy.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The MRI findings show complete absence of the corpus callosum, confirmed by Probst bundles and abnormal lateral ventricles, diagnosing Corpus Callosal Agenesis (CCA). This absence is definitive and supported by the clinical presentation of global developmental delay.

<Step 2> Subependymal gray matter nodules were detected on MRI, confirming gray matter heterotopia. Bilateral posterior globe defects and vitreous herniation indicate coloboma, which are common associations with CCA.

<Step 3> CCA is often linked with broader syndromic conditions like CHARGE or Dandy-Walker malformation, but the patient lacks physical or clinical features to support a specific syndromic diagnosis. No genetic or chromosomal analysis was performed.
<Step 4> CCA with gray matter heterotopia often leads to seizures due to disrupted brain connectivity, but the patient had no history of seizures up to the age of 1 year.
<Step 5> The final diagnosis focuses on the structural abnormalities (CCA, heterotopia, coloboma) identified on imaging, correlating with the patient's global developmental delay.
<Step 6> The patient was diagnosed with Corpus Callosal Agenesis, associated with subependymal gray matter heterotopia and bilateral eye coloboma, explained by MRI imaging findings.
<Step 7> Supportive care and regular follow-up were implemented based on the patient's needs.
